questions.specification:
Morales and Jensen (2020) investigated how stigma affects willingness to seek treatment for depression. Their aim was to examine whether negative attitudes toward therapy predicted lower treatment-seeking behaviour.

A sample of 400 university students completed anonymous surveys about their attitudes toward mental health treatment and whether they had ever sought therapy. Questions assessed perceived stigma (“Most people think less of someone who goes to therapy”) and personal stigma (“I would feel embarrassed if I sought therapy”).

Results showed that high stigma—especially personal stigma—was strongly associated with lower willingness to seek treatment. 

[Note: Fictionalised but based on Corrigan’s stigma research.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, perspective, responsibility, change.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Self-report subject to social desirability; sample limited to students.
Perspective: University students’ stigma may differ from older or clinical populations.
Responsibility: Researching stigma requires sensitivity; risk of reinforcing stereotypes.
Change: High stigma linked to lower treatment-seeking—interventions could reduce stigma over time.

questions.specification:
**Source 1:**
Graph showing results of a study in which students from different countries were asked to complete an anxiety scale (scores converted to percentage of students showing high anxiety symptoms). All participants used social media for at least 3 hours per day over the past month.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/06df936a-3f4a-44a0-80e4-2a9aaf4b7522)

**Source 2:**
Researchers conducted semi-structured interviews with 12 university students who reported struggling with mental health. Interviews focused on their experiences with social media. Responses were transcribed and analysed using thematic analysis.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7af7b1c1-83a9-4e13-b45b-271e1f13e060)

**Source 3:**
A psychology undergraduate wanted to explore the relationship between social media use and depression. They surveyed 100 students from their university, all of whom they personally knew to be frequent users of Instagram or TikTok. Students self-reported their average hours of social media use per day and completed a self-rated depression scale.

**Source 4:**
Researchers investigated the relationship between daily social media use (in hours) and self-reported loneliness (score from 1 to 100) among 30 university students.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/eea4ea15-c490-4def-aabc-58b035d06338)
*r(28) = 0.854, p = 0.007*

parts.content: Explain one issue that limits the interpretation of the data in **source 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a relevant issue.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explanation of why this limits interpretation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking explicitly to the claim.

Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks>.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Cultural differences (UK vs Brazil vs South Korea) not controlled → anxiety may reflect cultural stigma/reporting style, not social media.
Correlational, not causal → cannot conclude that social media caused anxiety.
Sampling limitation (all participants already 3+ hours/day users) → excludes light or non-users.

parts.content: Analyse the findings from **source 2** and state a conclusion linked to the claim that social media use may have a negative effect on students’ mental health.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for correct analysis, clear conclusion and explicit links to claim.

1–2 marks: Descriptive summary of one or two themes without analysis.

3–4 marks: Identification and explanation of how themes suggest social media may negatively affect mental health.

5–6 marks: Well-developed analysis linking multiple themes to mechanisms of negative effects, with balanced acknowledgement.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
FOMO, sleep disruption, and image pressure → strong evidence of negative outcomes.
Positive support from communities → protective factor.
Conclusion: social media can both harm and support mental health, but negative aspects more prominent in this sample.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could avoid bias in the study described in **source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for identification of bias, explanation of effects, and valid strategies. 

1–2 marks: Simple identification of bias.

3–4 marks: Some explanation of how bias affects results.

5–6 marks: Clear, well-developed discussion of strategies to minimise bias.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Researchers personally knew participants → social desirability bias; could use anonymous surveys or third-party recruitment.
Only students from one university, all frequent users → sampling bias; use random sampling across multiple institutions.
Self-reported depression and social media hours → measurement bias; use validated scales and digital logs of actual screen time.

parts.content: To what extent are the findings of **source 3** transferable to other populations or contexts?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of transferability and acknowledgement of both values and limitations. 

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Population limits: Sample restricted to one university and specific age group (likely 18–22) → limits transfer to other age groups (e.g., adolescents, adults).
Cultural limits: Study conducted in one cultural context → may not generalise to cultures where social media use patterns differ.
Platform focus: Frequent Instagram/TikTok users only; findings may not transfer to other platforms (e.g., LinkedIn, Twitter) or to light/occasional users.
Contextual limits: Conducted in an academic environment; transferability to non-student populations is limited.
Potential strengths: Large enough sample size (N=100) for a student study, which may provide some transfer within similar populations (e.g., university students in similar context

parts.content:
Using at least three of the sources above (sources 1-4), and your own knowledge, answer the following question:
To what extent can we conclude that the use of social media may have negative effects on students’ mental health?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for a well-developed response with critical analysis, clear links between multiple sources and the claim, and a consistent conclusion.

1–3 marks: Very limited knowledge and understanding of the sources and/or the claim. Minimal or inaccurate reference to sources. Largely descriptive, with little or no analysis. Psychological terminology absent or misused.

4–6 marks: Some knowledge and understanding of the claim. Reference to at least one source, but not always accurate or integrated. Limited analysis, with few links between sources and the claim. Some appropriate psychological terminology, but often vague.

7–9 marks: Reasonable knowledge and understanding of the claim, with reference to at least two sources. Some analysis and evaluation of research, though uneven or lacking development. Links between sources are attempted but may be simplistic. Psychological terminology is mostly accurate.

10–12 marks: Clear knowledge and understanding of the claim. Effective use of at least three sources, accurately applied to the question. Analysis and evaluation are present, with some critical thinking. Sources are compared or synthesised in addressing the claim. Psychological terminology is used appropriately.

13–15 marks: Comprehensive knowledge and understanding of the claim. Well-developed, balanced evaluation of the claim using at least three sources. Clear integration of sources with additional relevant knowledge. Critical analysis consistently developed (e.g., weighing strengths/limitations, contrasting findings, discussing generalisability). A well-reasoned and consistent conclusion. Precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Support for the claim: Source 1 (meta-analysis of correlational studies): links between heavy social media use and higher reported depressive symptoms. Source 3 (survey of frequent Instagram/TikTok users): self-reports show correlation between hours online and depression scale scores.
Additional knowledge: research on social comparison, body image, and cyberbullying supports negative impact.
Challenges to the claim: Source 2 (experimental study on active vs passive use): active, meaningful engagement may improve well-being. Source 4 (longitudinal study of adolescents): effects are small and depend on context, suggesting claims of harm may be overstated.
Additional knowledge: bidirectionality — depression may lead to more social media use, not just the reverse.
Conceptual considerations: Causality: many findings are correlational; cannot establish direct cause-effect. Bias: self-report measures vulnerable to demand/social desirability. Perspective: must consider cultural and generational differences in usage. Responsibility: ethical implications of overstating harm, which may stigmatise youth.

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of responsibility applies to the use of animal models in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of responsibility or animal models.
Mentions animals in vague terms.
Little to no evaluation of ethical responsibilities.
Weak or irrelevant links to health & well-being.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of responsibility in animal research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Rosenzweig & Bennett on enriched environments, Meaney on maternal care and stress response).
Identifies ethical responsibilities (minimizing harm, justification of use).
Some evaluation of benefits vs. costs, but limited depth.
Partial conclusion.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of responsibility and animal models.
Explains multiple studies (Rosenzweig & Bennett, Meaney, Rogers & Kesner on acetylcholine in rats).
Well-developed evaluation: ethical guidelines, 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement), balance between animal welfare and scientific benefit.
Clear links to implications for human health & well-being (mental health treatment, brain development).
Accurate terminology, balanced conclusion.

questions.specification: A community health clinic notices that patients with low serotonin levels often report persistent sadness and low motivation. Doctors are exploring whether this neurotransmitter might be linked to the development of depressive symptoms.

parts.content: Explain **one** biological explanation of depression.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a biological explanation and application to the scenario.

1–2 marks: Limited description of a biological explanation (e.g., “low serotonin causes depression”). Minimal application.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description (e.g., serotonin hypothesis, genetic predispositions). Partial application to depressive symptoms.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation (e.g., serotonin hypothesis with supporting research, neurogenesis theory, twin studies on heritability). Well-developed application to why patients show symptoms.

**Acceptable responses:**
Serotonin hypothesis (Caspi et al., SSRIs supporting evidence).
Genetic predisposition (concordance rates in twin studies).

questions.specification: A community health centre investigates how stigma affects people seeking treatment for depression. They notice that clients often worry about being judged negatively by others if they disclose their condition.

parts.content: Explain **one** study of stereotypes and its connection to this situation.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a relevant study and its application.

1–2 marks: Limited description stereotypes. Minimal application to stigma in treatment.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of stereotypes (definition, schema theory or SIT). Reference to a secondary study. Partial application to health-related stigma.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanation of stereotypes (stereotype threat, spotlight anxiety). Accurate and detailed description of a secondary study with clear findings. Strong application to the role of stereotypes in shaping treatment-seeking behaviour.

**Acceptable responses:**
Steele & Aronson (1995) — stereotype threat.
Spencer et al. (1999) — gender stereotypes in performance.

parts.content: Evaluate the role of bias in genetic research in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of bias or genetic research.
Mentions genes (e.g., twin/adoption studies) but with inaccuracies.
Little or no evaluation of bias (sampling, cultural, publication bias).
Superficial or irrelevant links to health & well-being.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of bias in genetic research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Kendler on depression, Caspi et al. on 5-HTT gene).
Identifies some biases (e.g., genetic determinism, population validity).
Partial analysis of how bias affects conclusions about health & well-being.
Some use of terminology, limited conclusion.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of bias in genetic research.
Accurate explanation of multiple studies (e.g., Caspi et al. 2003, Kendler 2006, twin/adoption research).
Well-developed evaluation of biases:
Sampling (twins not representative)
Ethical issues (stigma, determinism)
Publication bias
Gene–environment interactions.
Links bias clearly to implications for mental health treatment and well-being.
Accurate terminology, reasoned conclusion.

parts.content: Evaluate the concept of responsibility in research on stereotypes in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of responsibility in stereotype research.
Mentions stereotypes vaguely with little accuracy.
Response is descriptive, little or no link to health & well-being.
No meaningful evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of responsibility in stereotype research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Steele & Aronson on stereotype threat).
Explains issues of responsibility (e.g., psychological harm, ethical sensitivity, informed consent).
Some evaluation of research practices.
Conclusion present but not fully developed.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of ethical responsibility in stereotype research.
Accurate reference to multiple studies (e.g., Steele & Aronson, Spencer et al., Croizet & Claire).
Explains researcher responsibility for avoiding harm, stigma, or reinforcing stereotypes.
Well-developed evaluation of ethics, societal implications, and validity.
Accurate psychological terminology, reasoned and balanced conclusion.

questions.specification: A technology company evaluates how stereotype threat may affect women’s performance in coding assessments. They find that when gender stereotypes are highlighted, women’s scores decrease compared to when the task is presented neutrally.

parts.content: Suggest the role of stereotypes and/or stereotype threat in explaining the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a relevant study and its application.

1–2 marks: Limited description stereotypes. Minimal application to womens' performance in assignment.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of stereotypes (definition, schema theory or SIT). Reference to a secondary study. Partial application to the scenario outlined.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanation of stereotypes (stereotype threat, spotlight anxiety). Accurate and detailed description of a secondary study with clear findings. Strong application to the role of stereotypes in influencing how women perform in coding assignments.

**Acceptable responses:**
Steele & Aronson (1995) — stereotype threat.
Spencer et al. (1999) — gender stereotypes in performance.

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of perspective applies to research on emotion and memory in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of perspective (e.g., does not distinguish biological, cognitive, or sociocultural explanations).
Mentions emotion and memory vaguely (e.g., “people remember emotional events better”).
Minimal or no use of relevant research (e.g., Brown & Kulik).
Weak or no connection to health & well-being (e.g., PTSD, resilience).

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate understanding of perspective in emotion-memory research.
At least one study explained (e.g., Brown & Kulik on flashbulb memory; Sharot on 9/11; Cahill & McGaugh on adrenaline).
Shows some connection between perspective and findings:
Biological (hormonal/neurochemical explanation of memory consolidation).
Cognitive (schemas, reconstruction).
Sociocultural (shared cultural narratives).
Links to health & well-being (e.g., emotion-memory perspective helps explain resilience or vulnerability to PTSD).
Some evaluation of strengths/limitations of focusing on one perspective.
Conclusion present but underdeveloped.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong understanding of multiple perspectives in emotion-memory research.
Studies integrated accurately (Brown & Kulik, Sharot, Cahill & McGaugh, Neisser & Harsch).
Clear comparison of perspectives:
Biological perspective (role of hormones like adrenaline in memory consolidation).
Cognitive perspective (schemas reconstruct memories, even of emotional events).
Sociocultural perspective (cultural differences in recalling traumatic/flashbulb events).
Explicit link to health & well-being (e.g., importance of perspective in understanding memory distortions in trauma, therapeutic approaches).
Well-developed evaluation (no single perspective fully explains emotion and memory; integrative approach is stronger).
Balanced conclusion explicitly linking concept (perspective) and context (health & well-being).

parts.content: Evaluate the concept of causality in studies of emotion and memory in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited understanding of causality in emotion-memory studies.
Mentions “emotion affects memory” vaguely.
Little/no evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of causality in emotion and memory.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Brown & Kulik flashbulb memories, Cahill & McGaugh on adrenaline).
Explains difficulties in establishing causality (emotion as confounding, correlational findings).
Some evaluation of strengths/limitations.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of causality issues in emotion-memory research.
Multiple studies (Brown & Kulik, Cahill & McGaugh, Sharot on 9/11 memories).
Well-developed evaluation: correlational vs. experimental evidence, role of hormones, ecological validity.
Clear conclusion on extent to which causality can be established.
Accurate terminology, balanced conclusion.

questions.specification: A workplace researcher interviews employees about stressful incidents on the job. Many describe forgetting certain details or later recalling them differently from how they initially reported them.

parts.content: Explain the role reconstructive memory may play.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a relevant study and its application.

1–2 marks: Limited description of a reconstructive memory. Minimal application.

3–4 marks: Developed explanantion of reconstructive memory. Support from another study (e.g., Bartlett, Loftus & Palmer). Application to workplace stress is partial.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanation of reconstructive memory. Detailed description of a study with clear findings. Strong application to why employees recall details differently over time.

**Acceptable responses:**
Bartlett’s “War of the Ghosts” → memory shaped by cultural schemas.
Loftus & Palmer (car crash study) → wording of questions influences recall.

questions.specification:
**Source 1:**
A research team interested in looking at dieting behaviour in young people conducted a survey of nearly 450 university students, all aged between 18 and 24. The students came from the USA, Japan, and France. The survey was simple, participants simply had to indicate how frequently they engaged in dieting behaviour on a Likert scale; lower scores indicated less dieting behaviour. In the table below, “mean dieting frequency” refers to the number of new diets begun in the last 5 years; “% Reporting Restrictive Dieting in Past Year” refers to the number of individuals self-reporting a calorie consumption below the recommended daily intake, at least several days a month:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/567c1e77-1f6d-41a6-8868-ade27379e3f1)

**Source 2:**
The research team from Source 1 decided that they would like to do some qualitative research to gain further insight into the findings. They chose 15 volunteers, 5 from each of the countries referenced in Source 1. The researchers then conducted focus groups with each of the three groups. During the focus groups, researchers gave all volunteers an opportunity to share their thoughts. The individuals were asked questions like, “Why do you diet?”, “How long have you been dieting?”, among others. Each focus group had two researchers who took note of what participants said. The researcher teams then came up with short conclusions that summarise the main reasons for dieting in each country group:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/07178922-7e54-4589-9fc0-1482e75c99e9)

**Source 3:**
A correlational study of 200 adolescents (ages 15–18) measured cultural orientation (1–7, higher = more collectivist attitudes) and attitudes toward restrictive dieting (1–10, higher = stronger approval of dieting).
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/654904a7-b0af-4bf1-9e33-2a5496b18acf)
*r(198) = 0.54, p < 0.01*

**Source 4:**
An experiment tested whether cultural framing influenced food choice. 120 female university students were randomly assigned to one of two conditions:
Individualist frame: “Choosing healthy foods helps you achieve your personal health goals.”
Collectivist frame: “Choosing healthy foods helps you stay lean; it’s important for you friends and family that you are in-shape and healthy.
Participants were then asked to select lunch items, choosing between a low-calorie option and a higher-calorie option. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5ea365e8-ebbd-4f46-b928-932426d49f9b)

parts.content: Explain one issue that limits the interpretation of the data in **Source 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a limitation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why it limits interpretation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explicitly linking to claim.

Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks>.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Cultural reporting bias → dieting may be under/overreported due to stigma.
Self-report → participants may exaggerate or misremember.
Cross-sectional → snapshot only; doesn’t show long-term dieting patterns.
We also don’t know how many participants were in each country group, perhaps the restriction percentage for the US looks high simply because there were more individuals in that group.

parts.content: Analyse the findings from **Source 2** and state a conclusion linked to the claim that cultural values influence attitudes toward eating and dieting.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for correct analysis, clear conclusion and explicit links to claim.

1–2 marks: Descriptive summary of one or two themes without analysis.

3–4 marks: Identification and explanation of how the themes link to claim.

5–6 marks: Well-developed analysis linking multiple themes to claim. Analysis considers multiple perspectives; is well-balanced. 

**Acceptable answers may include**:
USA: Dieting tied to individualistic values and body image → supports cultural influence.
France: Food framed as enjoyment → dieting less necessary.
Japan: Dieting is linked to conformity and harmony.
Conclusion: Cultural values shape not only the frequency but also the meaning of dieting.

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could avoid bias in the study described in **Source 3**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for identification of bias, explanation of effects, and valid strategies. 

1–2 marks: Simple identification of bias.

3–4 marks: Some explanation of how bias affects results.

5–6 marks: Clear, well-developed discussion of strategies to minimise bias.

**Acceptable answers may include**:
Social desirability may affect self-reported dieting attitudes. Ensure anonymity and confidentiality to reduce pressure to give “acceptable” answers.
Random sampling: Ensure participants are selected randomly across different schools, communities, and socioeconomic groups to reduce sampling bias.

parts.content: To what extent are the findings of **Source 3** transferable to other populations or contexts?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of transferability and acknowledgement of both values and limitations. 

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Population differences: The study only includes adolescents aged 15–18. Findings may not apply to younger children, adults, or older populations.
Cultural specificity: If participants are from a single country or cultural background, the collectivism-dieting link may differ in other societies.
Contextual factors: The social environment, media exposure, and school culture could influence dieting attitudes

parts.content: Discuss how the researcher could improve the credibility of the findings in **Source 3**. [6]
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for understanding of credibility and suggestions for improvement.

1–2 marks: Limited description of transferability, vague reference to findings, little detail.

3–4 marks: Some consideration of transferability, partially accurate or unbalanced.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed discussion of transferability with well-developed evaluation.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
Increase sample size: A larger and more diverse sample strengthens statistical power and reduces the impact of outliers.
Replication: Repeat the study in different locations or age groups to see if the correlation holds.
Control confounding variables: Include factors such as socioeconomic status, media exposure, and BMI that could influence dieting attitudes, to ensure the correlation isn’t spurious.

parts.content: To what extent can we conclude that cultural values influence attitudes toward eating and dieting?
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for a well-developed response with critical analysis, clear links between multiple sources and the claim, and a consistent conclusion.

1–3 marks: Very limited knowledge and understanding of the sources and/or the claim. Minimal or inaccurate reference to sources. Largely descriptive, with little or no analysis. Psychological terminology absent or misused.

4–6 marks: Some knowledge and understanding of the claim. Reference to at least one source, but not always accurate or integrated. Limited analysis, with few links between sources and the claim. Some appropriate psychological terminology, but often vague.

7–9 marks: Reasonable knowledge and understanding of the claim, with reference to at least two sources. Some analysis and evaluation of research, though uneven or lacking development. Links between sources are attempted but may be simplistic. Psychological terminology is mostly accurate.

10–12 marks: Clear knowledge and understanding of the claim. Effective use of at least three sources, accurately applied to the question. Analysis and evaluation are present, with some critical thinking. Sources are compared or synthesised in addressing the claim. Psychological terminology is used appropriately.

13–15 marks: Comprehensive knowledge and understanding of the claim. Well-developed, balanced evaluation of the claim using at least three sources. Clear integration of sources with additional relevant knowledge. Critical analysis consistently developed (e.g., weighing strengths/limitations, contrasting findings, discussing generalisability). A well-reasoned and consistent conclusion. Precise use of psychological terminology throughout.

**Acceptable responses may include**:
**Source 1**: The data for source 1 indicates differences in dieting and restrictive behaviour surrounding food, for participants from different countries. This lends more weight to the claim that cultural values DO influence attitudes surrounding eating.
**Source 2**: There were differences however, in what exactly motivated individuals from each culture to hold their specific beliefs about dieting. Whereas individuals from the USA felt a personal desire to “look better,” for those from Japan, it was a matter of fitting in with the group.
**Source 3**: Individuals holding more collectivist values were correlated with having more restrictive ideas surrounding food and diet. This might make sense. People in collectivist societies prioritise harmony and the in-group, it may be important to then physically align with the in-group too. There may also be more pressure to remain healthy because the way an individual looks influences how others view their entire family. 
**Source 4**: Framing the participant’s choice for food played a role in the lunch option they chose. And it seems, collectivist framing seems to play a greater role in food choice, comparatively.
Own knowledge: Research on thin-ideal internalisation (Western media). Cultural psychology on food norms (Mediterranean vs Western diets). Emic vs etic approaches in studying eating.
Conclusion: Strong evidence that cultural values shape dieting attitudes, but influence differs: body image pressures in individualist cultures vs conformity/family values in collectivist ones.

questions.specification: A workplace wellness program introduces mindfulness sessions to reduce employee stress. After several weeks, researchers observed changes in brain activity patterns and improved well-being among participants.

parts.content: Explain **one** principle of neuroplasticity and how it may play a role.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of neuroplasticity and application to the mindfulness program.

1–2 marks: Limited description of neuroplasticity (e.g., “the brain can change”). Minimal application.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description (e.g., new neural connections can form through repeated practice). Partial application to mindfulness.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanation (e.g., synaptic plasticity, cortical remapping, strengthened connections through repetition). Strong application to stress reduction and improved well-being.

**Acceptable responses:**
Long-term potentiation (strengthened pathways).
Functional plasticity (brain adapts activity to new demands).

parts.content: Discuss how the concept of responsibility applies to cognitive research in the context of health & well-being.
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of responsibility in cognitive research.
Mentions ethical issues vaguely.
Weak or no examples of studies.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of responsibility in cognitive research.
Refers to at least one study (e.g., Brown & Kulik on flashbulb memories of traumatic events, Stroop task on stress).
Identifies some ethical issues (informed consent, potential distress).
Some evaluation but limited depth.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of responsibility in cognitive research.
Multiple studies (Brown & Kulik, Loftus on implanted memories, Stroop/working memory under stress).
Well-developed evaluation of ethical responsibility: minimizing harm, respecting confidentiality, debriefing.
Clear links to health & well-being (impact on trauma patients, stress research).
Accurate terminology, well-reasoned conclusion.

questions.specification:
Tanaka and Johnson (2016) tested whether a new therapy program reduced anxiety in post-surgical patients. The aim was to compare its effectiveness against standard care and to evaluate both self-reported and physiological indicators of anxiety.

Patients recovering from surgery (N = 60) were randomly assigned to receive either the therapy or standard care. The therapy consisted of six one-hour sessions that included relaxation training, guided imagery, and coping strategies. Anxiety was assessed using both self-report questionnaires and physiological measures, such as heart rate and blood pressure.

Results showed that patients in the therapy group reported lower anxiety and also demonstrated reduced heart rates compared to the control group. However, some patients reported discomfort with probing therapy questions, and others dropped out. The researchers concluded that the therapy showed promise.

[Note: Fictionalised but consistent with real anxiety intervention studies.]

parts.content: Discuss this study with reference to two or more of the following concepts: bias, change, measurement, responsibility.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>15 marks</marks> for discussion of the study with explicit reference to at least two concepts.

1–5 marks: Limited knowledge of the study. Minimal or inaccurate references to concepts. Mostly descriptive, little analysis.

6–10 marks: Some accurate knowledge of the study. At least one concept applied with some accuracy. Partial analysis, links partly relevant.

11–15 marks: Clear and detailed knowledge of the study. At least two concepts accurately and explicitly applied. Well-developed analysis of study strengths/limitations in relation to concepts. Reasoned conclusion consistent with arguments.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Bias: Teacher reports of “integration” subjective, risk of stereotyping. Word lists may have cultural bias.
Change: Memory strategies shifted as children integrated—illustrates adaptation over time.
Measurement: Free recall tasks may not reflect real-world learning; low ecological validity.
Responsibility: Testing newly arrived children could be stressful—researchers must balance insight with protection.

questions.specification:
A researcher compares identical and fraternal twins to investigate whether social anxiety may run in families. The study reports higher concordance rates for identical twins than for fraternal twins.
Explain how genetics may play a role.

parts.content: Explain how genetics may play a role.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of genetic influences and application to twin study scenario.

1–2 marks: Limited description of genetic influence (e.g., “it can be inherited”). Minimal application.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description (e.g., twin studies measure heritability). Partial application to concordance rates.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanation with terminology (e.g., MZ vs. DZ twins share different amounts of DNA; higher concordance suggests genetic basis, but environment must also be considered). Strong application to social anxiety.

**Acceptable responses:**
Twin study method (heritability estimates).
Gene-environment interaction (diathesis-stress model).

parts.content: To what extent does the concept of change explain the effectiveness of biological treatments in the context of health & well-being?
parts.markscheme:
**Level 0 (0 marks):**
Work does not reach a standard described below.

**Level 1 (1–5 marks):**
Limited knowledge of change or biological treatments.
Mentions medication vaguely (e.g., antidepressants).
Weak or inaccurate links to health & well-being.
Minimal evaluation.

**Level 2 (6–10 marks):**
Adequate knowledge of change and biological treatments.
Refers to at least one treatment (e.g., SSRIs for depression, antipsychotics for schizophrenia).
Explains how treatments produce change (e.g., serotonin reuptake).
Some evaluation of effectiveness (e.g., Kirsch’s placebo findings).
Partial conclusion.

**Level 3 (11–15 marks):**
Strong knowledge of change in biological treatments.
Multiple treatments/studies explained (SSRIs: Kirsch et al., Leucht on antipsychotics, ECT).
Well-developed evaluation of effectiveness (short-term vs. long-term, side effects, placebo effects, ethics).
Clear links to health & well-being outcomes (quality of life, symptom reduction).
Accurate terminology, reasoned conclusion.

questions.specification: At a mental health clinic, patients who have completed trauma therapy are asked to recall distressing events. Some remember the events with vivid detail, while others report gaps or distortions in their memories.

parts.content: Explain how emotion may influence memory.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of emotion’s role in memory and application to the scenario.

1–2 marks: Limited description of emotion and memory (e.g., “strong emotions affect memory”). Minimal application to trauma recall.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description (e.g., flashbulb memory theory, amygdala’s role in encoding). Partial application to vivid vs. distorted recall.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation (e.g., traumatic events may be encoded more strongly due to stress hormones, but also lead to repression/fragmentation). Well-developed application with correct use of terminology.

**Acceptable responses:**
Flashbulb memory theory (Brown & Kulik, later Neisser critiques).
Role of adrenaline/cortisol in enhancing or impairing consolidation.
PTSD and fragmented recall (hippocampal/amygdala interaction).
